title: Korhelyleves
subtitle: Hungarian sauerkraut and sausage soup
excerpt: Smoked sausage and sauerkraut simmered in paprika broth, finished with soured cream. Hungary's traditional hangover soup, sharply sour, smoky, and thick enough to mean business.
sourceNotes: Korhelyleves (literally 'drunkard's soup') is the Hungarian version of a dish found across Central and Eastern Europe: a restorative soup made from sauerkraut and smoked meat, eaten on New Year's Day or after celebrations to recover from the previous night. The combination of fermented cabbage, smoked meat, and paprika is also found in Polish kapuśniak, Slovak kapustnica, and Romanian ciorbă de varză. The soured cream stirred in at the end is the Hungarian marker.

content.text: Korhelyleves is everything a recovery soup should be: sharp, smoky, fatty, and hot. The sauerkraut provides the sourness; the smoked sausage and lardons provide the fat and smokiness; the paprika gives the broth its red colour and warmth. The soured cream stirred in at the end rounds everything off without losing the essential sharpness.

This soup improves overnight. Made on New Year's Eve and reheated on New Year's Day, it is considerably better than it would be served fresh. The sauerkraut softens further, the smoked meat flavour deepens into the broth, and the paprika loses its raw edge.

items.name: Sauerkraut
items.prepNote: roughly chopped if in long strands

content.text: Cook the {{lardons}} in a large saucepan over medium heat until the fat renders, about 4 minutes. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{paprika-sweet}} and {{paprika-hot}} if using. Return to the heat and immediately add the {{stock-chicken}}. Add the {{sauerkraut}}, {{smoked-pork-sausage}}, {{bay-leaves}}, and {{marjoram}}. Bring to a simmer and cook for 30 minutes.

Remove the bay leaves. Whisk the {{plain-flour}} into the {{soured-cream}} until smooth. Ladle a spoonful of hot soup into the cream, whisk to temper, then stir the cream mixture into the soup. Simmer gently for 5 minutes. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Serve with bread.

items.fix: Always temper: whisk a ladleful of hot soup into the cream before adding to the pot. Then keep the heat at a gentle simmer, not a full boil
items.cause: Cream added to vigorously boiling soup without tempering

items.fix: A pinch of sugar, stirred in at the end, will round out excessive sourness without eliminating the character of the dish
Add sliced sausage in the last 10 minutes of cooking if you prefer it tender. Early addition gives more flavour to the broth but can make the sausage firm
items.cause: Added at the start and cooked too long

content.text: Korhelyleves is New Year's Day food in Hungary. It is made in quantity, often from the leftover smoked meats and sauerkraut that were part of the previous day's celebrations. The name is not a recipe but a statement about what the soup is for. Across Central Europe, a version of this soup exists in every country: the Polish kapuśniak, the Slovak kapustnica. All of them are made from the same principle, sauerkraut and smoked pork, the two ingredients that kept through winter, combined into the most restorative soup available.
